Pepper__bell___Bacterial_spot.description: Small, water-soaked, yellowish spots on leaves that eventually turn brown and create dead tissue. Can cause severe leaf drop and sun-scalded fruit.
Pepper__bell___Bacterial_spot.cause: Caused by Xanthomonas bacteria. It spreads rapidly in warm, humid, and rainy field conditions. Splashing water spreads it from soil to leaves.
Pepper__bell___Bacterial_spot.treatment: Avoid overhead watering. Practice crop rotation. Remove and burn severely infected plants immediately.
Pepper__bell___Bacterial_spot.medicine: Use Copper Oxychloride 50% WP (e.g., Tata Rallis Blitox, Crystal Blue Copper) OR Copper Hydroxide (e.g., DuPont Kocide 2000). Combine with a bactericide like Streptomycin Sulphate (e.g., Aries Plantomycin, Tagmycin).

Pepper__bell___healthy.description: The leaf structure is vibrant green, firm, and shows no signs of lesions, bacterial spotting, or pest damage.
Pepper__bell___healthy.cause: Optimal soil health, good irrigation practices, and effective preventative care.
Pepper__bell___healthy.treatment: Maintain current agricultural practices. Ensure proper drainage to prevent root rot.
Pepper__bell___healthy.medicine: No treatment required. Continue standard fertilizer and micronutrient schedule.

Potato___Early_blight.description: Brown or black spots on older leaves with distinct dark concentric rings (like a target board). Lower leaves yellow and drop off.
Potato___Early_blight.cause: Caused by the fungus Alternaria solani. Thrives in high humidity and temperatures above 24°C. Spores survive in soil debris.
Potato___Early_blight.treatment: Prune lower leaves to improve air circulation. Apply mulch to prevent soil spores from splashing onto the plant.
Potato___Early_blight.medicine: Apply Mancozeb 75% WP (e.g., Indofil M-45, Dhanuka M-45), Chlorothalonil 75% WP (e.g., Syngenta Kavach), or Azoxystrobin (e.g., Syngenta Amistar).

Potato___Late_blight.description: Large, dark brown/black water-soaked lesions on leaves, often with a white fungal growth on the underside during humid conditions. Can destroy a field in days.
Potato___Late_blight.cause: Caused by the water mold Phytophthora infestans. Spreads incredibly fast in cool, wet weather.
Potato___Late_blight.treatment: Destroy infected plants immediately. Do not leave infected tubers in the field after harvest.
Potato___Late_blight.medicine: Urgent: Spray Metalaxyl + Mancozeb (e.g., Syngenta Ridomil Gold, Tata Master), Cymoxanil combos (e.g., Corteva Curzate), or Ametoctradin (e.g., BASF Zampro).

Potato___healthy.description: Foliage is healthy, green, and intact. No signs of blight lesions or yellowing.
Potato___healthy.cause: Good field management and favorable weather conditions.
Potato___healthy.treatment: Continue regular monitoring, especially when weather turns cool and wet.
Potato___healthy.medicine: No treatment required. Maintain preventative sprays only if district risk is high.

Tomato_Bacterial_spot.description: Small, dark, greasy spots on leaves and stems. Spots on fruit become raised and scabby.
Tomato_Bacterial_spot.cause: Caused by Xanthomonas bacteria species. Seed-borne and spread by splashing rain or overhead irrigation.
Tomato_Bacterial_spot.treatment: Use disease-free seeds. Avoid working in the field when foliage is wet. Drip irrigation is highly recommended.
Tomato_Bacterial_spot.medicine: Use Copper Oxychloride 50% WP (e.g., Tata Rallis Blitox, IFFCO MC Copper) OR Copper Hydroxide (e.g., DuPont Kocide 2000). Combine with Streptomycin Sulphate (e.g., Aries Plantomycin).

Tomato_Early_blight.description: Irregular brown spots with concentric rings on older, lower leaves. Causes premature defoliation and sunscald on fruit.
Tomato_Early_blight.cause: Fungus Alternaria solani. Worsened by heavy dew, frequent rain, and poor plant nutrition.
Tomato_Early_blight.treatment: Stake plants to keep foliage off the ground. Remove infected bottom leaves.
Tomato_Early_blight.medicine: Apply Mancozeb 75% WP (e.g., Indofil M-45, Uthane M-45), Chlorothalonil 75% WP (e.g., Syngenta Kavach, Tata Ishaan), or Difenoconazole (e.g., Syngenta Score).

Tomato_Late_blight.description: Pale green to brown spots on leaves, developing a white fuzzy growth on the underside. Stems develop dark lesions.
Tomato_Late_blight.cause: Phytophthora infestans. Same pathogen that caused the Irish Potato Famine. Requires wet, cool conditions.
Tomato_Late_blight.treatment: Immediate removal of infected plants. Ensure excellent field drainage and weed control.
Tomato_Late_blight.medicine: Urgent: Spray Metalaxyl + Mancozeb (e.g., Syngenta Ridomil Gold, UPL Unilax), Cymoxanil combos (e.g., Corteva Curzate), or Fluopicolide (e.g., Bayer Infinito).

Tomato_Leaf_Mold.description: Pale greenish-yellow spots on the upper leaf surface with olive-green to brown velvety fungal growth on the underside.
Tomato_Leaf_Mold.cause: Fungus Passalora fulva. Extremely common in greenhouses or high tunnels with poor ventilation and high humidity.
Tomato_Leaf_Mold.treatment: Increase row spacing and prune aggressively to improve air circulation. Keep greenhouse humidity below 85%.
Tomato_Leaf_Mold.medicine: Apply Propiconazole 25% EC (e.g., Syngenta Tilt, Crystal Radar) or standard preventative Mancozeb/Chlorothalonil.

Tomato_Septoria_leaf_spot.description: Numerous small, circular spots with dark borders and tan/gray centers. Heavily infected leaves turn yellow and drop.
Tomato_Septoria_leaf_spot.cause: Fungus Septoria lycopersici. Spores splash from the soil onto lower leaves during rain.
Tomato_Septoria_leaf_spot.treatment: Use thick organic mulch to create a barrier between soil and leaves. Do not compost infected debris.
Tomato_Septoria_leaf_spot.medicine: Treat with Chlorothalonil 75% WP (e.g., Syngenta Kavach) or Propiconazole 25% EC (e.g., Syngenta Tilt) starting when the first fruits form.

Tomato_Spider_mites_Two_spotted_spider_m.description: Leaves appear stippled (tiny yellow/white dots). Severe infestations show fine, silky webbing on the undersides of leaves, turning them bronze.
Tomato_Spider_mites_Two_spotted_spider_m.cause: Microscopic arachnids (Tetranychus urticae). They thrive in hot, dry, dusty field conditions.
Tomato_Spider_mites_Two_spotted_spider_m.treatment: Reduce field dust. Keep plants well-watered. Introduce natural predators like ladybugs if possible.
Tomato_Spider_mites_Two_spotted_spider_m.medicine: Apply specific miticides: Abamectin 1.9% EC (e.g., Crystal Abacin), Spiromesifen 22.9% SC (e.g., Bayer Oberon), or Bifenthrin 10% EC (e.g., FMC Talstar).

Tomato__Target_Spot.description: Small, dark brown spots with light brown centers on leaves. Fruit develops deeply sunken, dark lesions with concentric rings.
Tomato__Target_Spot.cause: Fungus Corynespora cassiicola. Favors high humidity, moderate temperatures, and extended periods of leaf wetness.
Tomato__Target_Spot.treatment: Improve airflow through pruning. Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization, which creates dense canopies.
Tomato__Target_Spot.medicine: Spray Propiconazole 25% EC (e.g., Syngenta Tilt) or Azoxystrobin (e.g., Syngenta Amistar).

Tomato__Tomato_YellowLeaf__Curl_Virus.description: Severe stunting of the plant. Leaves cup upward, turn yellow at the margins, and become noticeably crumpled and reduced in size.
Tomato__Tomato_YellowLeaf__Curl_Virus.cause: A viral disease transmitted entirely by the Silverleaf Whitefly (Bemisia tabaci).
Tomato__Tomato_YellowLeaf__Curl_Virus.treatment: Infected plants cannot be cured and must be uprooted and destroyed immediately. Focus on vector control.
Tomato__Tomato_YellowLeaf__Curl_Virus.medicine: Control whiteflies using Imidacloprid (e.g., Bayer Confidor, Tata Mida) or Thiamethoxam (e.g., Syngenta Actara). Use yellow sticky traps.

Tomato__Tomato_mosaic_virus.description: Leaves show a mottled light and dark green mosaic pattern. Plants are stunted, and fruits may be distorted with internal browning.
Tomato__Tomato_mosaic_virus.cause: A highly contagious virus that survives in seed, soil, and debris. Easily spread by workers' hands and contaminated tools.
Tomato__Tomato_mosaic_virus.treatment: No cure exists. Pull and burn infected plants. Wash hands with soap and disinfect pruners thoroughly before touching healthy plants.
Tomato__Tomato_mosaic_virus.medicine: No chemical treatment available. Focus strictly on sanitation and planting resistant varieties.

Tomato_healthy.description: Leaves are a rich, even green with no mottling, spots, or curling. Stems are sturdy.
Tomato_healthy.cause: Excellent agronomic practices, balanced nutrition, and good pest management.
Tomato_healthy.medicine: No treatment required. Use Neem oil spray periodically as a preventative organic measure against pests.
